questions.answer: Additive decomposition: Y = Trend + Seasonal + Residual. The seasonal component has a constant amplitude regardless of the trend level. Appropriate when seasonal fluctuations remain roughly the same size over time. Multiplicative decomposition: Y = Trend * Seasonal * Residual. The seasonal component is proportional to the trend level. Appropriate when seasonal fluctuations grow (or shrink) as the overall level of the series changes. For the retail sales scenario: multiplicative decomposition is correct. If the business is growing (increasing trend) and holiday spikes are growing proportionally larger (Black Friday in year 1 is +$10K over baseline, in year 5 is +$50K over a 5x-larger baseline), the seasonal effect is proportional to the level, not additive. Using additive decomposition would underestimate seasonal effects in recent periods and overestimate them in early periods because it assumes the same absolute spike size regardless of the baseline. A practical test: if plotting the series shows the seasonal amplitude increasing with the level, use multiplicative. If constant amplitude, use additive. Taking the log of a multiplicative series converts it to additive.

rubric.key: Correctly defines both decomposition types with formulas
Chooses multiplicative with correct reasoning about proportional growth
Explains consequence of wrong choice and log transformation shortcut

questions.question: Why must time series cross-validation respect temporal ordering? Describe the expanding window cross-validation method and how it differs from standard k-fold.
questions.answer: Standard k-fold cross-validation randomly shuffles data into folds, which means future observations can appear in the training set while past observations appear in the test set. For time series, this creates data leakage: the model sees the future during training, producing unrealistically optimistic performance estimates that do not reflect real forecasting conditions. Expanding window cross-validation respects time: Fold 1: Train on months 1-12, test on month 13. Fold 2: Train on months 1-13, test on month 14. Fold 3: Train on months 1-14, test on month 15. ... and so on. Each fold trains on all available history up to the test point and evaluates on the next unseen time period. The training set grows with each fold (expanding window). Alternative: sliding window uses a fixed training size (e.g., always 12 months of training), dropping old data as new data enters. Key differences from k-fold: (1) Test sets are always after training sets temporally. (2) Folds are not randomly created. (3) Training sets grow (or slide). (4) Results reflect actual forecasting conditions where you train on the past and predict the future.

rubric.key: Explains data leakage from shuffling temporal data
Correctly describes expanding window with specific fold examples
Contrasts key differences from standard k-fold

questions.question: A fraud detection model has the following test set results: Precision = 0.85, Recall = 0.40, F1 = 0.54. The business wants to catch at least 70% of fraud (recall >= 0.70). What changes would you make to the model, and what trade-off would you expect?
questions.answer: The model is precise (85% of flagged transactions are actually fraud) but has low recall (only catching 40% of actual fraud). To increase recall to 70%: Primary approach: Lower the classification threshold. The model outputs a probability score. Currently the threshold might be 0.5 (flag as fraud if probability > 50%). Lowering it to, say, 0.3 means more transactions are flagged, catching more real fraud (higher recall) but also flagging more legitimate transactions (lower precision). Expected trade-off: Precision will decrease, perhaps to 0.50-0.65. This means more false positives (legitimate transactions flagged for review), increasing operational cost for the fraud review team. Secondary approaches: (1) Retrain with class-weight adjustment to penalize missing fraud more than false alarms. (2) Apply SMOTE to oversample fraud cases in training. (3) Engineer additional features that help distinguish fraud from legitimate transactions. (4) Try ensemble methods (Random Forest, XGBoost) that may find better decision boundaries. The business must decide the acceptable precision level: if the fraud review team can handle 2x the volume, a lower threshold is the simplest solution.

rubric.key: Identifies threshold adjustment as the primary approach
Correctly predicts precision decrease as the trade-off
Suggests additional model improvements and business context consideration
Quantifies the operational impact of more false positives

questions.question: Explain the difference between L1 (Lasso) and L2 (Ridge) regularization in regression. When would you choose each, and what happens to features with Lasso that does not happen with Ridge?
questions.answer: Both add a penalty term to the regression loss function to prevent overfitting. Ridge (L2) adds lambda * sum(beta_i^2): penalty proportional to the sum of squared coefficients. Ridge shrinks all coefficients toward zero but never sets them exactly to zero. All features remain in the model with reduced influence. Choose Ridge when you believe all features are relevant (or most are), and you want to reduce multicollinearity and overfitting without feature elimination. Lasso (L1) adds lambda * sum(|beta_i|): penalty proportional to the sum of absolute coefficient values. Lasso shrinks coefficients toward zero AND can set some exactly to zero. This performs automatic feature selection. Features with zero coefficients are effectively removed from the model. Choose Lasso when you have many features and suspect only a subset are truly relevant, or when you want an interpretable model with fewer features. What Lasso does that Ridge does not: drives coefficients to exactly zero, producing a sparse model. With 100 features, Lasso might select 15 as important and zero out 85. Ridge would keep all 100 with small but non-zero coefficients. Elastic Net combines both penalties (alpha * L1 + (1-alpha) * L2) and is often the best default choice.

rubric.key: Correctly defines both penalty formulas and their effects
Identifies Lasso's unique feature selection (coefficients exactly zero)
Provides appropriate use cases for choosing each

questions.question: You run k-means clustering with k=5 on customer data. The silhouette scores for each cluster are: [0.72, 0.68, 0.15, 0.71, 0.65]. What does the low score for cluster 3 indicate, and what should you investigate?
questions.answer: The silhouette score ranges from -1 to 1. A score of 0.15 for cluster 3 means its members are, on average, barely more similar to their own cluster than to the nearest neighboring cluster. This indicates cluster 3 is poorly defined, overlapping significantly with one or more adjacent clusters. Investigations: (1) Visualize cluster 3 in 2D using PCA: are its points scattered between two other clusters rather than forming a cohesive group? (2) Examine which neighboring cluster has the most overlap. Perhaps cluster 3 should be merged with that neighbor (try k=4 and compare overall silhouette). (3) Profile cluster 3's characteristics: if its members have widely varying attributes, k-means may be forcing diverse points into one cluster because k is too low, or the data has a non-spherical structure that DBSCAN would handle better. (4) Check for outliers within cluster 3 that are poorly assigned. (5) Try running k-means with different random seeds (n_init parameter) to verify cluster 3 is stable and not an artifact of initialization. A single poorly-defined cluster does not invalidate the other four. Consider keeping the well-defined clusters and treating cluster 3 members as 'unclassified' for further investigation.

rubric.key: Correctly interprets low silhouette as poor cluster cohesion
Suggests visualization and overlap analysis with neighbors
Proposes actionable next steps (merge, change k, try different algorithm)

questions.question: Explain TF-IDF and why it is more useful than raw word counts for text classification. Calculate the TF-IDF score for the word 'algorithm' appearing 3 times in a document of 100 words, given that 'algorithm' appears in 10 out of 10,000 documents in the corpus.
questions.answer: TF-IDF combines Term Frequency (how often a word appears in a document) with Inverse Document Frequency (how rare the word is across the corpus). Raw word counts are problematic because common words like 'the' and 'is' dominate, despite carrying little information. TF-IDF downweights common words and upweights distinctive words. Calculation: TF = 3/100 = 0.03 (word frequency in the document). IDF = log(10000/10) = log(1000) = 3.0 (using natural log: ln(1000) = 6.91, or log10(1000) = 3.0). TF-IDF = TF * IDF = 0.03 * 3.0 = 0.09 (with log10) or 0.03 * 6.91 = 0.207 (with natural log). The high IDF reflects that 'algorithm' is rare (only in 0.1% of documents), so it is very informative when it appears. A word like 'the' appearing in most documents would have IDF near 0, giving it a low TF-IDF regardless of frequency. This makes TF-IDF better for classification because distinctive words (those that characterize a specific topic or sentiment) naturally receive higher weights.

rubric.key: Correctly explains TF-IDF and why it improves over raw counts
Correct TF and IDF calculation
Explains how the score reflects word distinctiveness

questions.question: A machine learning model for hiring decisions is found to have a 15% lower approval rate for female applicants compared to male applicants with similar qualifications. The model does not use gender as a feature. How is this possible, and what should be done?
questions.answer: How this is possible despite not using gender directly: (1) Proxy variables: features that correlate with gender serve as indirect proxies. Examples: name (gendered names), university major (gender-skewed fields), years of experience (career gap patterns), hobbies, pronoun usage in resumes. The model learns to use these proxies even without the gender feature. (2) Historical bias in training data: if the company historically hired fewer women, the training labels encode that bias. The model learns to replicate historical hiring patterns, which include the gender disparity. (3) Feature engineering bias: features like 'leadership experience' or 'prestigious internships' may reflect structural advantages that correlate with gender. What should be done: (1) Audit: Compute disparate impact ratio (female approval rate / male approval rate). A ratio below 0.8 violates the four-fifths rule used in US employment law. (2) Identify proxy features: Use SHAP values or permutation importance separately for male and female applicants to find features driving the disparity. (3) Mitigation: Apply fairness constraints during training (equalized odds), reweight training data, or adjust thresholds per group. (4) Remove or transform the strongest proxy features if they do not have legitimate business justification. (5) Ongoing monitoring: Continuously audit approval rates by gender in production. (6) Human oversight: Flag borderline decisions for human review rather than relying entirely on the model.

rubric.key: Identifies proxy variables as the mechanism for indirect discrimination
Mentions historical bias in training labels
Proposes concrete mitigation steps including audit, proxy identification, and fairness constraints
Recommends ongoing monitoring and human oversight

questions.question: Your pandas analysis on a 500MB CSV takes 30 seconds. The production dataset is 50GB. Should you switch to PySpark? What alternatives would you consider first, and how would you decide?
questions.answer: Not necessarily. Spark has significant overhead (cluster setup, serialization, task scheduling) that makes it slower than pandas for data that fits on a single machine. For 50GB, consider alternatives first: (1) Polars: single-machine parallel DataFrame library in Rust, handles 10-50GB efficiently without distributed overhead. Often 5-10x faster than pandas on the same hardware. Try this first. (2) Chunked pandas: read the CSV in chunks (pd.read_csv(chunksize=100000)), process each chunk, and aggregate. Works if the analysis is reducible to per-chunk operations. (3) DuckDB: SQL-based analytics engine that processes larger-than-memory data efficiently. Install as a Python package and query directly on Parquet files. (4) Column selection: if you only need 5 of 50 columns, read only those columns (usecols parameter). Switching to Parquet format reduces file size 5-10x and enables column pruning. (5) Upgrade hardware: a machine with 128GB RAM handles 50GB in pandas comfortably (data + working memory). Cloud VMs with high memory are cheap. Decision framework: Try Polars or DuckDB first (single-machine, no distributed complexity). If the data exceeds 100GB or grows continuously, invest in Spark. The operational complexity of a Spark cluster is only justified when single-machine tools genuinely cannot handle the scale.

rubric.key: Does not reflexively recommend Spark, explains overhead concerns
Suggests at least two single-machine alternatives (Polars, DuckDB, chunked pandas)
Provides a clear decision framework for when to use Spark
